vocabulary.def: A structured way of solving problems by starting with the people who have the problem, not the solution
Understanding a problem from another person's point of view, not your own
A rough early version of a product used to test ideas before building the real thing
The person who will actually use the product
Improving a design based on feedback, then testing again

lessonSlides.title: Get the Room Ready
lessonSlides.roleModel: Reshma Saujani (born 1975) is the founder of Girls Who Code, which has taught over 500,000 girls to code across the United States. She started it after noticing that while boys were encouraged to take risks and fail, girls were taught to be perfect — which made them afraid to try coding. Her insight was not technical. It was human: she understood the problem from the user's point of view before designing the solution. That is exactly what design thinking is.
whatToDo: Write these five words on the board — leave them visible all lesson.
Write the five design thinking stages on the board with arrows: EMPATHISE → DEFINE → IDEATE → PROTOTYPE → TEST
Put out sticky notes — one per student — for the Phase 1 spark activity.
Have at least 6 sheets of paper per group ready for the prototype sprint.
lessonSlides.tip: Arrange desks into groups of three before students arrive if possible. The prototype sprint works best when groups are already seated together.
boardWords: Design Thinking — solving problems by starting with the person who has the problem, not the solution
Empathy — understanding a problem from another person’s point of view, not your own
Prototype — a rough early version used to test ideas before building the real thing
User — the person who will actually use the product
Iteration — improving a design based on feedback, then testing again

lessonSlides.title: Spark Question — What Actually Annoys You?
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Think about something that genuinely annoys you or wastes your time at school or at home. Not something you want — something that is actually a problem for you or someone you know. What is it?
whatToDo: Ask every student to write one problem on a sticky note silently — no discussion yet. Give them 90 seconds.
Ask 5–6 students to read theirs aloud. Write them on the board without comment.

Tell them: 'Every piece of technology that exists today started with someone noticing a problem exactly like the ones you just wrote. The people who built Google were annoyed that searching the internet was slow. The people who built Spotify were annoyed that buying music was expensive. Today you start in the same place they did — with a real problem.'
Leave all the sticky notes on the board — groups will use them in Phase 4 to choose their design challenge.
lessonSlides.tip: The silent sticky note step is important — it gives every student, including quieter ones, a chance to contribute before anyone speaks. Do not skip this. Collect and display all notes even if some seem trivial.

lessonSlides.title: Warm-Up — The Broken School Day
lessonSlides.whatToSay: I am going to describe a school day that has several small problems in it. Your job is to spot every problem — not to suggest a solution yet. Just the problem.
whatToDo: Read this scenario aloud slowly: 'Maya arrives at school and cannot remember which classroom her first lesson is in because the schedule changed this week. She checks the noticeboard but it has not been updated. She is late. In the canteen at lunch, she queues for 10 minutes and when she gets to the front, the meal she wanted is sold out — but nobody told the people in the queue. After lunch she needs to return a library book but the library is closed with no sign saying when it will reopen. On her way home she realises she left her PE kit in her locker but does not know if she will need it tomorrow.'

Ask pairs to list every problem they heard. Take answers and write them on the board. You should get at least five: schedule changes not communicated; canteen does not warn when items run out; library times not displayed; forgotten kit; general communication gap.
Then ask: 'Which of these could technology help with? And which is not really a technology problem — it is a people or organisation problem?'
lessonSlides.tip: If a student immediately says 'just make an app for it,' push back: 'Before we design the app — what exactly is the problem? Who has it? When does it happen? How often? Why does it happen?' Those questions are the core of the empathy stage.

lessonSlides.title: Concept Introduction — Design Thinking and the Logic Behind Screens
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Professional designers use a process called design thinking to make sure they are solving the right problem in the right way. It has five stages, but today we focus on the first three — because those happen before any building starts.
whatToDo: Point to the board: EMPATHISE → DEFINE → IDEATE → PROTOTYPE → TEST
Explain each of the first three in plain language:

EMPATHISE: understand the problem from the user's point of view. Ask — who is affected? When does it happen? How does it make them feel? What have they already tried? You find this out by talking to and watching people. You do not assume you already know.

DEFINE: write a clear problem statement. Who has the problem, what it is, and why it matters. No solution yet. Weak: 'Students need an app.' Strong: 'Students in Groep 7 often arrive late because they have no reliable way to check an updated timetable quickly.'
IDEATE: generate as many possible solutions as you can without judging any of them. Quantity, not quality. Bad ideas are welcome — they sometimes lead to good ones.

Draw a phone outline on the board. Draw two buttons: 'Check timetable' and 'Report an issue.' Draw an arrow from 'Check timetable' to a second screen. Say: 'That arrow means: when you tap this button, the app takes you to this screen. This is the logic of the app — the same conditional logic from Lesson 3. IF the user taps this button, THEN show this screen.'

Write on the board: The app uses sequences / loops / conditionals / variables / could use AI / needs encryption. Say: 'You are not just designing a pretty picture. You are designing a system. Every screen has logic behind it — and that logic is built from exactly the concepts you have learned.'
lessonSlides.tip: Spend two minutes explicitly naming which lessons connect to which design feature. Students who can say 'this button is a conditional from Lesson 3' are integrating knowledge, which is the goal of this session.

lessonSlides.title: Choose Your Problem
whatToDo: Groups of three look at the sticky notes from Phase 1 and the problems identified in the warm-up.

Each group chooses one problem to solve. It must be: a real problem students or teachers actually experience; something where technology could genuinely help; specific enough that they can design a solution today.
If groups struggle, suggest these starting points from the warm-up: canteen stock problem, timetable communication, or lost property.
Once chosen, groups write the problem at the top of their first sheet of paper.
lessonSlides.tip: If two groups choose the same problem, that is fine — it will make the share at the end more interesting, not less. Do not redirect them.

lessonSlides.title: Empathy — Who Has This Problem?
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Before drawing anything, answer these four questions in writing. This is the empathy stage — we are not designing yet.
rules: Who exactly has this problem? Give them a name and describe them — for example 'Maya, age 11, Groep 7, always rushing in the morning'
When does the problem happen? Be specific — 'every Monday when the timetable changes'
What have they already tried that did not work? This prevents designing a solution that already exists and failed
How does it make them feel? Frustrated? Embarrassed? Stressed? This is the human dimension
whatToDo: Groups write their answers before drawing anything. Give them 4 minutes.
Walk around and check — if a group is already drawing screens, redirect them back to the questions.
lessonSlides.tip: The named person — 'Maya' — is called a persona in design thinking. Having a specific imaginary person makes design decisions clearer. When a group disagrees about a feature, they can ask: 'Would Maya use this?' That question is easier to answer than 'would users use this?'
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Reshma Saujani started here: she noticed girls were being left out of coding. She did not build an app first — she asked who has the problem. That is your first step too.

lessonSlides.title: Define — Write the Problem Statement
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Write one sentence that completes this template — and it must NOT mention a solution.
rules: '[Name] needs a way to [do what] because [why it matters], but currently [what makes it hard].'

Example: 'Maya needs a way to check her updated timetable quickly in the morning because arriving late to the wrong classroom is stressful, but currently the only option is checking a noticeboard that is often outdated.'
whatToDo: Groups write their problem statement. Give them 3 minutes.
Each group shares it with you before moving on. If it mentions a solution, send them back to rewrite it.
The problem statement must describe the problem, not the answer.
lessonSlides.tip: Common mistake: 'Maya needs an app that shows the timetable.' That is a solution, not a problem statement. Push them to remove the app entirely and describe only what Maya needs to be able to do.

lessonSlides.title: Ideate — Generate Solutions
lessonSlides.whatToSay: You have 3 minutes — write down every possible solution you can think of. No judging, no crossing out, just generating. Aim for at least 6 ideas. Wild ideas are welcome.
whatToDo: Set a visible timer for 3 minutes.
After 3 minutes, groups circle the one idea they want to prototype.
It does not have to be the most exciting — it should be the one they can actually sketch in the time available.
lessonSlides.tip: If a group has only 2 or 3 ideas, push them: 'What if there was no app — how would you solve it with just paper? What if you had unlimited budget? What if you had to solve it in one sentence?' Constraints generate ideas.
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Reshma's first ideas were not her best. Neither will yours be. Keep going — ideating means making the first idea a starting point, not a final answer.

lessonSlides.title: Prototype — Draw Three Screens
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Now draw your app on paper. You have exactly 10 minutes. Messy and functional is better than beautiful and incomplete.
rules: Exactly three screens — no more, no fewer
Each screen must have: a title, at least two interactive elements (buttons, input fields, lists), and arrows showing where each element takes the user
One screen must show what happens when something goes wrong — e.g. timetable not updated yet, or canteen run out of all options
whatToDo: Walk around and ask groups: 'What variable does this screen need to remember?', 'What conditional fires when the user taps this button?', 'What happens if the user has no internet connection?'
The third question introduces edge cases — the same concept from Lessons 3 and 5.
If groups are spending too long on layout and colours, redirect them: 'The drawing does not matter. The logic matters. Draw boxes and arrows.'
lessonSlides.tip: Time pressure is intentional. Professional designers use it to stop overthinking and start making. Remind groups: this is a rough draft, not a finished product.

lessonSlides.title: Discussion
whatToDo: Take 2–3 volunteer answers per question.
Question 2 generates the most useful discussion for this age group — if the class is engaged, let it run.
discussionQuestions: In Step 2 you described a specific person who has the problem. How did thinking about that person change the decisions you made when designing? What assumptions did you make about Maya that might not be true for everyone? Who might your design not work for?

We said the problem statement must not mention a solution. Why is that rule important? What goes wrong when you start with a solution instead of a problem? Think about an app that exists right now that feels like it was built by someone who never talked to the people who would use it — what did the designer get wrong?

Your prototype has three screens. A real app might have 300. At what point does more screens make a product better — and at what point does it make it worse? The original Instagram had exactly four screens: feed, camera, notifications, profile. What would you cut from your design if you could only keep two screens?
lessonSlides.tip: Question 3 introduces the concept of minimum viable product without needing the term. Students have strong intuitions about badly designed technology — give them vocabulary to describe why things feel wrong.

lessonSlides.title: The Logic Layer
lessonSlides.whatToSay: For each of your three screens, fill in the logic layer. This connects your visual design to the underlying code.
rules: Screen 1: [Screen name] | Variable: ___ | Conditional: IF ___ THEN ___ ELSE ___ | Edge case: What if ___?
Screen 2: [Screen name] | Variable: ___ | Conditional: IF ___ THEN ___ ELSE ___ | Edge case: What if ___?
Screen 3: [Screen name] | Variable: ___ | Conditional: IF ___ THEN ___ ELSE ___ | Edge case: What if ___?
whatToDo: Walk around and check variables are specific to the design — not just 'SCORE = 0' copied from Lesson 4, but something like 'TIMETABLE_UPDATED = true/false' or 'USER_CLASS = Groep 7'.
Check conditionals reflect real decisions the app needs to make.
Check edge cases show students thinking about what could go wrong, not just what should go right.
lessonSlides.tip: Students who can fill in all three rows have genuinely understood how the program concepts connect to real software design. This is the most important assessment moment in the lesson.

lessonSlides.title: The Pitch Sentence
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Each group writes one sentence using this formula. This is what you will use to open your presentation in Lesson 8.
rules: 'Our app is called [NAME]. It helps [WHO] solve [PROBLEM] by [FEATURE]. Without it, [CONSEQUENCE].'

Example: 'Our app is called SchoolCheck. It helps students in Groep 7 solve the problem of not knowing about timetable changes by sending a notification 30 minutes before school starts. Without it, students arrive at the wrong classroom and miss the start of their lesson.'
whatToDo: Groups write their pitch sentence on the back of their prototype.
If time allows, ask one or two groups to read theirs aloud.
lessonSlides.tip: This sentence doubles as the opening of the Lesson 8 presentation. Make sure every group has one written before the lesson ends.

lessonSlides.title: Stress Test Your Prototype
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Swap your prototype with another group. You have 5 minutes to try to break it — find every situation the design does not handle.
lessonSlides.task: Receiving group: write on a sticky note every gap or unclear decision you find. Return it with the prototype. Original group: respond to each point — either improve the design or explain why the edge case is acceptable. Questions to guide your review: What happens if the user does not have an account yet? What happens if the user makes a mistake and wants to go back? What happens if the user is in Groep 5, not Groep 7 — does it still work? What happens if the app loses internet connection halfway through?
challengeQuestions: How many edge cases did you find that the original group had not considered?
Did the original group's design change after your feedback — or did they defend it? What does each response tell you about the designer?
If you had to choose between fixing one edge case or adding one new feature, which would you choose and why?
lessonSlides.tip: Groups who receive challenging feedback and actually use it to change their design are demonstrating iteration — the fourth stage of design thinking. This is one of the most important signals in the entire program. Note those students.

lessonSlides.title: Close the Lesson
whatToDo: Each group holds up their prototype — just holds it up, no explanation. Give the class 30 seconds to look at each one silently.
Ask: 'Just from looking — without any explanation — what problem does each one seem to solve?' If the class can identify the problem from the design alone, the prototype is communicating clearly.
Then ask every student — verbally or on a sticky note — to complete this sentence.
lessonSlides.exitReflection: The hardest part of designing something was _______ because _______.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Common answers: 'not jumping to a solution,' 'figuring out what the user actually needs,' 'deciding what to leave out.' All of these are things professional designers say. Write the most honest one on the board.
lessonSlides.watchFor: Did groups write a problem statement that genuinely avoids mentioning a solution? Did the logic layer task produce variables and conditionals specific to their design — or did they copy generic examples from earlier lessons? Did any groups in the extension receive challenging feedback and actually use it to change their design rather than defend the original? That last behaviour — genuine willingness to revise — is one of the most important signals in the entire program.

title: Build Something Real - Design Thinking and Your First Prototype
description: Students integrate all prior concepts to define a real problem, design with empathy, and create a three-screen paper prototype with logic.

roleModelOfDay.name: Reshma Saujani
roleModelOfDay.intro: Founder of Girls Who Code, which has taught over 500,000 girls to code — built after she noticed technology was systematically excluding the people who could benefit most.

sloGoals.domain: Domain B — Digital Skills (22A, 22B) & Domain C — Digital Society (23A)
sloGoals.detail: This lesson targets design thinking and digital creation (22A, 22B): students apply all prior computational concepts to define a real problem, prototype a solution, and test it with users. They practise empathy-driven design and iterative improvement.
sloGoals.why: SLO Domain B (22A) requires students to design and create digital products with a purpose. This lesson bridges computational thinking with real-world application — students experience the full design process that underpins every app, game, and system they use.

learningObjectives: Identify a real, user-centered problem worth solving.
Apply empathise, define, and ideate stages of design thinking.
Produce a three-screen paper prototype with connected navigation.
Describe logic layer elements using variables, conditionals, and edge-case handling.
prerequisites: Builds on all previous lessons as an integration session.
fullDescription: Age group: Groep 5-8 (ages 8-12). Duration: 45-60 minutes. Devices required: none (fully unplugged). SLO alignment: Domain B (22A, 22B), Domain C (23A). Students move from concept understanding to real-world product design through empathy mapping, problem framing, ideation, and paper prototyping. Role model: Reshma Saujani, whose people-first insight shaped Girls Who Code and highlights that design starts with users, not features.

teacherInstructions.setup: Prepare sticky-note wall for problem collection and board space for design-thinking stages: Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test.
steps: Phase 1 (0-3 min): Spark question. Students silently write real annoyances on sticky notes and share selected examples.
Phase 2 (3-10 min): Broken school day warm-up. Identify problems before proposing solutions and discuss which are technology-solvable.
Phase 3 (10-20 min): Concept intro. Teach first three design-thinking stages, show wireframe and navigation arrows, and connect to prior lesson logic concepts.
Phase 4 (20-42 min): Paper prototype sprint in groups of three with timed steps: choose problem, empathy answers, problem statement, ideation, then three-screen prototype.
Phase 5 (42-47 min): Discussion on user assumptions, solution-first pitfalls, and minimum viable feature scope.
Exercises (47-57 min): Finish prototype, complete logic-layer worksheet, and write one-sentence pitch.
Share and reflect (52-57 min): Silent gallery scan, class infers problem from each prototype, and students complete reflection sentence.
discussionPrompts: How did designing for one named user change feature decisions?
Why must a strong problem statement avoid mentioning a solution?
When do extra screens improve a product and when do they reduce usability?
If you could keep only two screens, what would you cut and why?
tips: Protect the silent sticky-note step so every student contributes a real problem.
If groups jump to app ideas, bring them back to who, when, why, and how often.
Require problem statements to be solution-free before ideation starts.
During sketching, prioritize logic and edge-case handling over visual polish.
Use time pressure intentionally to encourage rough, testable prototypes.

teacherGuide: Core message: great technology starts with a real user problem, not a feature idea. Students should leave with a concrete prototype plus explicit logic notes that tie visual design to computational thinking foundations. Track whether groups revise based on feedback rather than defending first drafts.

studentExercises.title: Primary Exercise - Paper Prototype Sprint
studentExercises.description: In groups, complete a full mini design-thinking cycle and produce a 3-screen paper prototype with navigation and one error-state screen.
instructions: Choose one real school-life problem from sticky notes or warm-up list.
Empathise: answer who has this problem, when it happens, what they already tried, and how it feels.
Define: write one problem statement using template '[Name] needs a way to ... because ... but currently ...'.
Ideate: generate at least six solution ideas in three minutes, then select one to prototype.
Prototype exactly three screens with titles, at least two interactive elements each, arrows showing flow, and one explicit error or failure case.
successCriteria: Problem is specific, real, and user-centered.
Prototype has exactly three connected screens with clear navigation.
At least one edge-case or failure-state screen is included.

studentExercises.title: Written Task 1 - The Logic Layer
studentExercises.description: For each screen, document one variable, one IF/THEN/ELSE conditional, and one edge case to show system logic behind the design.
instructions: For Screen 1, write screen name, one meaningful variable, one conditional, and one edge case.
Repeat for Screen 2.
Repeat for Screen 3.
Use real app-specific variable names such as TIMETABLE_UPDATED or USER_CLASS, not generic placeholders.
Check that each conditional matches an actual interaction in your screen design.
successCriteria: All three screens have specific variables, conditionals, and edge cases.
Logic entries match the actual prototype behavior.
Group demonstrates connection between UI decisions and program logic.

studentExercises.title: Written Task 2 - The Pitch Sentence
studentExercises.description: Write a one-sentence Dragon's Den style pitch that states app name, user, problem, feature, and consequence without the app.
instructions: Use this formula: 'Our app is called [NAME]. It helps [WHO] solve [PROBLEM] by [FEATURE]. Without it, [CONSEQUENCE].'
Make sure WHO and PROBLEM match your empathy and define stages.
Keep sentence specific and testable, not vague.
Read sentence aloud and revise once for clarity.
Save this sentence as your opening for Lesson 8 presentation.
successCriteria: Pitch contains all five required elements.
Sentence aligns with prototype and problem statement.
Audience can understand value proposition in one read.

studentChallenges.title: Prototype Stress Test
studentChallenges.description: Swap prototypes with another group and try to break the design by finding confusing flows, missing states, or unsupported users.
instructions: Exchange prototype with another group.
For five minutes, list every failure point or unclear interaction you can find.
Frame feedback as 'What happens if ...?' questions.
Return prototype with feedback note.
Original group spends five minutes revising design or defending with clear rationale.
successCriteria: Feedback identifies concrete usability or logic gaps.
Receiving group can act on at least one suggestion.
Revision demonstrates iteration rather than cosmetic change only.

studentChallenges.title: Two-Screen MVP Cut
studentChallenges.description: Reduce your three-screen concept to a two-screen minimum viable product and justify what was removed.
instructions: Assume you are allowed to keep only two screens.
Choose which screen to remove.
Rewrite key navigation arrows and logic to maintain core value.
Explain what user need is still solved and what trade-off was accepted.
Share your cut decision with another group and compare priorities.
successCriteria: Group preserves the core problem-solving flow in two screens.
MVP decision is justified with clear reasoning.
